name: Epilogue
classification_notes: Epilogue is a clean, upright sans-serif with a familiar grotesque structure. The page describes it as crisp and familiar yet distinct, sitting within the grotesque tradition.
summary: A clean grotesque sans with nine weights and matching italics, available as a variable font and released under an Open Font License via Google Fonts.
description: Covering Thin through Black with a matching italic for each weight, Epilogue also ships as a single variable font allowing arbitrary weight values across the full 100–900 axis. Originally released in August 2018 under a different name, it gained additional weight coverage and a variable slant axis in its current form. It is available free on Google Fonts and GitHub under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: ETC Typefaces describe Epilogue as a clean, crisp go-to font that is familiar enough to be immediately legible yet distinct enough to be memorable. It was built to fill the gap between generic system fonts and bespoke display faces, covering everything from screen to print across a full range of weights and a variable font option.

name: Gluten
classification_notes: Gluten is a highly expressive, rounded display typeface with exaggerated curves and a variable slant axis. Its quirky personality and extreme weight range place it firmly in the display category rather than any functional text classification.
summary: A rounded, variable display typeface with a full weight range and a variable slant axis that can tilt forwards or backwards.
description: Released by ETC Typefaces in 2018, Gluten is built around exaggerated curves and a buoyant personality that the designers maintained across nine weights from Thin to Black. Alongside standard italics, it includes reverse italics for additional tilt, with the slant axis variable so type can lean forward, sit upright or lean back. Available as static desktop and web fonts as well as a single variable font file, and distributed freely under the SIL Open Font License via Google Fonts and GitHub.
foundry_blurb: ETC describe Gluten as delicious, loud, very round and 100% fun, noting the chief design challenge was stretching its personality across a full variable weight range without losing the quirky character present at its heaviest. It includes both standard and reverse italics, with a variable slant axis, and has been updated several times since its August 2018 release before becoming libre via Google Fonts under an Open Font License.

name: Anybody
classification_notes: A variable-width display grotesque drawing on Impact and Eurostile, spanning ultra-condensed to extra-expanded widths with a high x-height and low cap height optimised for headline use across extreme proportions.
summary: A 90-style variable grotesque spanning ExtraCondensed to ExtraExpanded, drawing on Impact and Eurostile for display work across extreme widths.
description: Released in 2018 and now freely available under the SIL Open Font License via Google Fonts, the family runs nine weights with italics across five width grades, totalling 90 static styles plus a single variable font. High x-height and low cap height exaggerate the extreme width settings, while a 10° italic angle keeps the slant noticeable but restrained. OpenType features extend its utility beyond pure headline use.
foundry_blurb: ETC Typefaces cite Impact and Eurostile as key references, mixed with a heavy dose of 90s inspiration. The name reflects the typeface's breadth: spanning UltraCondensed to ExtraExpanded, Anybody is designed to adapt to a wide variety of situations, from meme text to movie titles, without being limited to either. It has received several updates since its 2018 release and is now available in libre form with an Open Font License on Google Fonts and GitHub.

name: Sono
classification_notes: Sono is a soft geometric sans with a variable mono axis, spanning from proportional to fully monospaced; its rounded, low-cap-height character places it within the geometric sans tradition rather than a strict grotesque or humanist lineage.
summary: A soft geometric sans with a variable mono axis, spanning proportional and fully monospaced styles across seven weights.
description: Originally released in 2018 as a purely monospaced typeface, Sono gained a variable mono axis in 2022 that allows the letterforms to slide between monospaced and proportional spacing. The proportional styles are grouped under the Sans prefix. Available as static desktop and web fonts or as a single variable font, it is distributed free via Google Fonts and GitHub under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: ETC Typefaces describe Sono as named for its most obvious characteristics: soft and monospaced. It launched in 2020 with only monospaced styles but was updated in 2022 to include a mono axis and corresponding proportional Sans styles. It is the final free font the foundry intends to release for the foreseeable future, available on Google Fonts and GitHub.

name: Imbue
classification_notes: Imbue is a serif with crisp, curvy letterforms and hairline serifs at thin weights, designed with both text and display optical sizes, placing it firmly in the display serif category with variable font capabilities.
summary: A variable serif with weight and optical size axes, released in 2018 and now freely available under an Open Font License via Google Fonts and GitHub.
description: Built around two variable axes — weight and optical size — it ranges from hairline serifs at the thinnest settings to bolder, more substantial strokes, across nine named weights. The optical size axis spans text-optimised to display-optimised styles, though the foundry actively encourages mixing them out of their intended contexts. Its character coverage extends to broad Latin Extended, with OpenType features included.
foundry_blurb: ETC Typefaces describe Imbue as something crisp and curvy, made for a side project when nothing existing felt right. It features variable weight and optical size axes, static styles for desktop and web, and has been updated several times since its August 2018 release. Thanks to Google Fonts, it is now available under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Grandstander
classification_notes: A block-lettered display face with soft, slightly inflated forms inspired by children's books. Classified as display rather than sans-serif owing to its whimsical, illustrative character that prioritises personality over neutrality.
summary: A children's book-inspired display face with softly inflated letterforms, available as a variable font across nine weights with matching italics.
description: Designed with block lettering as its starting point, Grandstander introduces subtle outward bulges into otherwise straight forms, giving each glyph the slightly overinflated quality of balloon lettering. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with an italic, and is offered as both static styles and a single variable font with weight and slant axes. Released in August 2018 and subsequently updated, it is now freely available via Google Fonts and GitHub under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: ETC Typefaces describe Grandstander as a whimsically block-lettered yet crisp display face inspired by children's books, noting that subtle bends in traditionally straight forms give each glyph a warm, friendly quality, as if a conventional sans had been ever so slightly overinflated. Originally a single-weight design, it grew into a multi-weight variable font with both static and variable formats available, and now exists in libre form through Google Fonts and GitHub under an Open Font License.

name: Trispace
classification_notes: Trispace descends from League Mono but deliberately abandons strict monospacing, allowing narrow and wide glyphs to take slightly more or less space than the standard cell. This places it in a quasi-proportional or 'duospace' category adjacent to traditional monospaced fonts.
summary: A quasi-proportional sans derived from League Mono, available as a variable font with condensed and expanded widths across eight weights.
description: Trispace began as a rework of League Mono, retaining the rhythm and character of monospaced type while allowing the narrowest and widest glyphs to deviate slightly from a fixed cell width. The result is more readable than a strict monospace without losing its technical, code-adjacent feel. Released in 2018 and now distributed via Google Fonts under the Open Font License, it spans 24 static styles plus a single variable font covering weight and width axes.
foundry_blurb: ETC describe Trispace as growing out of League Mono, which the founder originally created before establishing Etcetera Type Co. Rather than a straight update, they reworked many letterforms and moved away from strict monospacing, letting narrow glyphs such as i, l and quotes take up slightly less space and wide glyphs such as M, W and Æ take up slightly more, preserving the monospace feel at a more legible level. The family is available as static styles and a variable font, released under the SIL Open Font License via Google Fonts and GitHub.

name: Tourney
classification_notes: Tourney is a geometric, sport-inflected display face with variable width and weight axes, inline/outline layering capability, and a pronounced technical character that places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A variable display face drawing on sport and science-fiction aesthetics, designed for layering across weight, width and italic axes.
description: Released in 2018 and available under an Open Font License via Google Fonts and GitHub, Tourney spans nine weights from Thin to Black across condensed, regular and expanded widths, each with matching italics. Its lightest weights read almost as outlines, thickening to a solid fill at the heaviest, which makes the family well suited to stacked or layered compositions. The dual inspiration from stadium graphics and spacecraft interfaces gives it a broadly useful range for bold, tech-adjacent or sporting contexts.
foundry_blurb: ETC Typefaces describe Tourney as a variable font with competing interests: outline versus inline, upright versus italic, condensed versus expanded, and solo versus stacked. Framed as a collision of tech and sport aesthetics, it would feel equally at home on a spaceship or in a stadium. The lightest weight functions almost as an outline, thickening to a solid mass at 900. It is available as static desktop and web fonts or as a single variable font, released under the SIL Open Font License.
